metadata.title: Guidance for Industry: Premarketing Risk Assessment

rules.rule_text: Sponsors seeking approval must provide from the clinical trials a body of evidence that adequately characterizes the product's safety profile.
rules.source_section: Section III: The Role of Risk Assessment in Risk Management
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors seeking approval must provide from the clinical trials a body of evidence that adequately characterizes the product's safety profile.

rules.rule_text: From the outset of development, sponsors must pay careful attention to the overall design of the safety evaluation, including targeting potential problems suspected from preclinical data or effects of related drugs.
rules.source_section: Section IV: Generating Risk Information During Clinical Trials
rules.verbatim_quote: To maximize the information gained from clinical trials, FDA recommends that from the outset of development, sponsors pay careful attention to the overall design of the safety evaluation.

rules.rule_text: The appropriate size of the safety database depends on the product's novelty, availability of alternative therapies, intended population and condition, and intended duration of use.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A: Size of the Premarketing Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: The appropriate size of a safety database supporting a new product will depend on a number of factors specific to that product, including: Its novelty (i.e., whether it represents a new treatment or is similar to available treatment); The availability of alternative therapies and the relative safety of those alternatives as compared to the new product; The intended population and condition being treated; The intended duration of use
rules.exceptions: Safety databases for life-threatening diseases with no alternative treatments are usually smaller.

rules.rule_text: For products intended for long-term treatment of non-life-threatening conditions (continuous treatment for 6 months or more), at least 1500 subjects should be exposed to the investigational product, with 300 to 600 exposed for 6 months and 100 exposed for 1 year.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A: Size of the Premarketing Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: For products intended for long-term treatment of non-life-threatening conditions, (e.g., continuous treatment for 6 months or more or recurrent intermittent treatment where cumulative treatment equals or exceeds 6 months), the ICH and FDA have generally recommended that 1500 subjects be exposed to the investigational product (with 300 to 600 exposed for 6 months, and 100 exposed for 1 year).
rules.exceptions: Products for life-threatening diseases may be approved with smaller databases. The 1500 should include only those exposed in multiple dose studies.

rules.rule_text: A safety database larger than 1500 patients may be required when there is concern that the drug would cause late-developing adverse events, when there is a need to quantitate an expected low-frequency serious adverse event, or when the benefit from the product is small or uncertain.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A: Size of the Premarketing Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: The E1A guidance describes a number of circumstances in which a safety database larger than 1500 patients may be appropriate, including the following: 1. There is concern that the drug would cause late developing adverse events, or cause adverse events that increase in severity or frequency over time. ... 2. There is a need to quantitate the occurrence rate of an expected specific low-frequency adverse event. ... 3. A larger database would help make risk-benefit decisions in situations when the benefit from the product: Is small (e.g., symptomatic improvement in less serious medical conditions)

rules.rule_text: A larger safety database is appropriate when the proposed treatment is for a healthy population (e.g., chemoprevention or preventive vaccine) or when an effective and safe alternative to the investigational product is already available.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A: Size of the Premarketing Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: In addition to the considerations provided in E1A, there are other circumstances in which a larger database may be appropriate. 1. The proposed treatment is for a healthy population (e.g., the product under development is for chemoprevention or is a preventive vaccine). 2. An effective alternative to the investigational product is already available and has been shown to be safe.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must communicate with the review division early in the development program (e.g., at pre-IND meeting) and revisit the issue at appropriate regulatory milestones (end-of-phase 2 and pre-NDA meetings) regarding appropriate safety database size.
rules.source_section: Section IV.A: Size of the Premarketing Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that sponsors communicate with the review division responsible for their product early in the development program (e.g., at the pre-IND meeting) on the appropriate size of the safety database. FDA also recommends that sponsors revisit the issue at appropriate regulatory milestones (e.g., end-of-phase 2 and pre-NDA meetings).

rules.rule_text: Efforts to ensure the quality and completeness of a safety database should be comparable to those made to support efficacy, including the use of standard terminology such as MedDRA.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B: Considerations for Developing a Premarketing Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: In general, efforts to ensure the quality and completeness of a safety database should be comparable to those made to support efficacy. Because data from multiple trials are often examined when assessing safety, it is particularly critical to examine terminology, assessment methods, and use of standard terms (e.g., use of the Medical Dictionary for Regulatory Activities (MedDRA)) to be sure that information is not obscured or distorted.

rules.rule_text: Controlled, long-term safety studies should be considered when a safety issue is identified during earlier development, especially when the safety issue of concern is more common with cumulative exposure.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.1: Long-Term Controlled Safety Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Although it is clear that long-term controlled studies will not usually be conducted, such studies may be particularly useful when a safety issue is identified during earlier development of the drug. In these cases, safety studies designed to test specific safety hypotheses may be appropriate. This would be especially true in situations where the safety issue of concern is more common with cumulative exposure.

rules.rule_text: Premarketing safety databases must include a population sufficiently diverse to adequately represent the expected target population, particularly in phase 3 studies, with only patients with obvious contraindications excluded.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.2: A Diverse Safety Database
rules.verbatim_quote: Premarketing safety databases should include, to the extent possible, a population sufficiently diverse to adequately represent the expected target population, particularly in phase 3 studies. ... To the extent feasible, only patients with obvious contraindications or other clinical considerations that clearly dictate exclusion should be excluded from study entry.

rules.rule_text: When dose is not established in phase 2, more than one dose level should be examined in phase 3 trials of fixed dose products to better characterize the relationship between product exposure and resulting clinical benefit and risk.
rules.source_section: Section IV.B.3: Exploring Dose Effects Throughout the Clinical Program
rules.verbatim_quote: When a dose is not established in phase 2, more than one dose level should be examined in phase 3 trials of fixed dose products to better characterize the relationship between product exposure and resulting clinical benefit and risk.
rules.exceptions: Phase 3 trials do not necessarily need to examine a range of doses if phase 2 studies have reasonably established the single most appropriate dose.

rules.rule_text: Risk assessment programs must examine unanticipated interactions during clinical trials, including drug-drug interactions beyond known metabolic pathways, product-demographic relationships, product-disease interactions, and product-dietary supplement interactions.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C: Detecting Unanticipated Interactions as Part of a Safety Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: risk assessment programs should examine a number of interactions during controlled safety and effectiveness trials and, where appropriate, in specific, targeted safety trials. This examination for unanticipated interactions should include the potential for the following: Drug-drug interactions in addition to those resulting from known metabolic pathways ... Product-demographic relationships — by ensuring sufficient diversity of the population (including sex, age, and race) ... Product-disease interactions — by ensuring sufficient variability in disease state and concomitant diseases ... Product-dietary supplement interactions for commonly used supplements that are likely to be co-administered

rules.rule_text: Systematic incorporation of pharmacokinetic assessments into later phase clinical trials should be used to detect unexpected PK interactions and to aid in the detection of unexpected relationships between pharmacokinetics and adverse events.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C: Detecting Unanticipated Interactions as Part of a Safety Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: One important way to detect unexpected relationships is by systematic incorporation of pharmacokinetic (PK) assessments (e.g., universal steady state sampling or population PK analyses) into some or all of the later phase clinical trials, including any specific safety trials. PK assessments can aid in the detection of unexpected PK interactions and, in some cases, could suggest exposure-response relationships for both safety and efficacy.

rules.rule_text: When a product has well-established, valid biomarkers pertinent to a known safety concern, the marker must be studied during PK studies and clinical development.
rules.source_section: Section IV.C: Detecting Unanticipated Interactions as Part of a Safety Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: When a product has one or more well-established, valid biomarkers pertinent to a known safety concern, the marker should be studied during the PK studies and clinical development (e.g., creatine phosphokinase assessments used in the evaluation of new HMG CoA reductase inhibitors as a marker for rhabdomyolysis, or assessments of QT/QTc effects for new antihistamines).

rules.rule_text: For products intended to be chronically used with dose-related toxicities, sponsors should examine whether a lower or less frequent maintenance dose would be appropriate.
rules.source_section: Section V.A: Risk Assessment During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: If a product is intended to be chronically used (particularly when it has a very long half-life) and/or has dose-related toxicities, it can be useful to examine whether a lower or less frequent maintenance dose would be appropriate.

rules.rule_text: When a drug has the potential for adverse effects not likely to be detected or readily reported by patients without special attention (e.g., CNS effects, sexual function effects), additional testing or specific assessments within existing trials must be performed.
rules.source_section: Section V.A: Risk Assessment During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: Certain kinds of adverse effects are not likely to be detected or readily reported by patients without special attention. When a drug has the potential for such effects, additional testing or specific assessments within existing trials may be appropriate. For example, for a new drug with recognized CNS effects (especially sedating effects), sponsors should conduct an assessment of cognitive function, motor skills, and mood.

rules.rule_text: If a product is to be studied in pediatric patients, special safety issues must be considered, including effects on growth and neurocognitive development for very young children, safety of excipients, and universal immunization requirements.
rules.source_section: Section V.A: Risk Assessment During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: If a product is to be studied in pediatric patients, special safety issues should be considered (e.g., effects on growth and neurocognitive development if the drug is to be given to very young children/infants; safety of excipients for the very young; universal immunization recommendations and school entry requirements for immunization).

rules.rule_text: The NDA for all new small molecule drugs must address: drug-related QTc prolongation, drug-related liver toxicity, drug-related nephrotoxicity, drug-related bone marrow toxicity, drug-drug interactions, and polymorphic metabolism.
rules.source_section: Section V.C: Addressing Safety Aspects During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends addressing the potential for the following serious adverse effects as a part of the new drug application (NDA) for all new small molecule drugs: Drug-related QTc prolongation; Drug-related liver toxicity; Drug-related nephrotoxicity; Drug-related bone marrow toxicity; Drug-drug interactions; Polymorphic metabolism
rules.exceptions: A drug intended to be topically applied may be shown to have no systemic bioavailability; therefore systemic toxicities would be of no practical concern. Not all items apply to biological products.

rules.rule_text: For biological products such as cytokines, antibodies, and other recombinant proteins, immunogenicity must be assessed, including the incidence and consequences of neutralizing antibody formation and the potential for adverse events related to binding antibody formation.
rules.source_section: Section V.C: Addressing Safety Aspects During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: Potentially important issues for biological products include assessments of immunogenicity, both the incidence and consequences of neutralizing antibody formation and the potential for adverse events related to binding antibody formation.

rules.rule_text: For gene-based biological products, safety assessment must include transfection of nontarget cells, transmissibility of infection to close contacts, and genetic stability for long-persistence transfections.
rules.source_section: Section V.C: Addressing Safety Aspects During Product Development
rules.verbatim_quote: For gene-based biological products, transfection of nontarget cells and transmissibility of infection to close contacts, and the genetic stability of products intended for long-persistence transfections constitute important safety issues.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must ensure that each investigator's verbatim adverse event terms are coded to standardized preferred terms using a single coding convention or dictionary (e.g., MedDRA) throughout a clinical program.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A: Describing Adverse Events to Identify Safety Signals
rules.verbatim_quote: FDA recommends that sponsors ensure that each investigator's verbatim terms are coded to standardized, preferred terms specified in a coding convention or dictionary. ... In general, FDA suggests that sponsors use one coding convention or dictionary (e.g., MedDRA) throughout a clinical program

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must not use more than one version of a coding dictionary within the same analysis and must not analyze adverse event data using one version and then base proposed labeling on a different version.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.1: Accuracy of Coding
rules.verbatim_quote: It is not advisable to analyze adverse event data using one version and then base proposed labeling on a different version.
rules.exceptions: If use of a single version is not possible, sponsors must appropriately group and analyze adverse events taking into account revisions in subsequent versions.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must explore the accuracy of adverse event coding with respect to both investigators and the persons who code adverse events, including confirming that verbatim terms have been appropriately coded and identifying any instances where serious terms may have been inappropriately mapped to more benign terms.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.1: Accuracy of Coding
rules.verbatim_quote: We recommend that in addition to ensuring that investigators have accurately characterized adverse events, sponsors confirm that verbatim terms used by investigators have been appropriately coded. Sponsors should strive to identify obvious coding mistakes as well as any instances when a potentially serious verbatim term may have been inappropriately mapped to a more benign coding term, thus minimizing the potential severity of an adverse event.

rules.rule_text: Prior to analyzing a product's safety database, sponsors must ensure that adverse events were coded with minimal variability across studies and individual coders.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.1: Accuracy of Coding
rules.verbatim_quote: Prior to analyzing a product's safety database, sponsors should ensure that adverse events were coded with minimal variability across studies and individual coders.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must prospectively group adverse event terms and develop case definitions or use accepted standardized definitions whenever possible, particularly for syndromes such as serotonin syndrome, Parkinsonism, and drug withdrawal.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.2: Coding Considerations During Adverse Event Analysis
rules.verbatim_quote: sponsors, in consultation with the Agency, should prospectively group adverse event terms and develop case definitions or use accepted standardized definitions whenever possible. A prospective grouping approach is particularly important for syndromes such as serotonin syndrome, Parkinsonism, and drug withdrawal, which are not well characterized by a single term.
rules.exceptions: Some groupings can be constructed only after safety data are obtained, at which time consultation with FDA might be considered.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must explain adverse event groupings explicitly in their applications so that FDA reviewers have a clear understanding of what terms were grouped and the rationale for the groupings.
rules.source_section: Section VI.A.2: Coding Considerations During Adverse Event Analysis
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should explain such groupings explicitly in their applications so that FDA reviewers have a clear understanding of what terms were grouped and the rationale for the groupings.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors should analyze event rates by dose for clinically important adverse events that may be product related and for events expected based on a product's pharmacologic class or preclinical data.
rules.source_section: Section VI.C: Analyzing Dose Effect as a Contribution to Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: Sponsors should analyze event rates by dose for clinically important adverse events that may be product related and events that might be expected based on a product's pharmacologic class or preclinical data.

rules.rule_text: When pooling safety data, sponsors must confirm that study designs, ascertainment methods, and measurement strategies employed in the studies that are pooled are reasonably similar.
rules.source_section: Section VI.D: Role of Data Pooling in Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: To ensure that pooling is appropriate, sponsors should confirm that study designs, as well as ascertainment and measurement strategies employed in the studies that are pooled, are reasonably similar.

rules.rule_text: All placebo-controlled studies in a clinical development program should be considered and evaluated for appropriateness for inclusion in a pooled safety analysis.
rules.source_section: Section VI.E: Using Pooled Data for Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: All placebo-controlled studies in a clinical development program should be considered and evaluated for appropriateness for inclusion in a pooled analysis.
rules.exceptions: Phase 1 pharmacokinetic and pharmacodynamic studies should generally be excluded from pooled analyses.

rules.rule_text: In pooled analyses, the risk of the safety outcome must be expressed in reference to total person-time (exposure time) or evaluated using a time-to-event analysis when the duration of drug exposure varies across subjects.
rules.source_section: Section VI.E: Using Pooled Data for Risk Assessment
rules.verbatim_quote: The risk of the safety outcome of interest should be expressed in reference to total person-time (exposure time) or be evaluated using a time-to-event analysis. When the duration of drug exposure for the individual subjects included in a pooled analysis varies, sponsors should not express the risk merely in terms of event frequency (that is, using persons as the denominator).

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must ascertain the reasons for all dropouts and withdrawals from clinical trials, including whether a safety issue was a part of the reason for withdrawal, and must follow participants with serious safety issues until resolution or stabilization.
rules.source_section: Section VI.F: Rigorous Ascertainment of Reasons for Withdrawals from Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: sponsors should attempt to account for all dropouts. Sponsors should try to ascertain what precipitated dropout or withdrawal in all cases, particularly if a safety issue was a part of the reason for withdrawal. ... Participants who leave a study because of serious or significant safety issues should be followed closely until the adverse events are fully and permanently resolved or stabilized (if complete resolution is not anticipated), with follow-up data recorded in the case report forms.

rules.rule_text: Sponsors must include information on discontinuations due to abnormal laboratory values, vital signs, or ECG findings that are not characterized as adverse events, in addition to information on discontinuations due to adverse events.
rules.source_section: Section VI.F: Rigorous Ascertainment of Reasons for Withdrawals from Studies
rules.verbatim_quote: Some patients withdraw due to abnormal laboratory values, vital signs, or ECG findings that are not characterized as adverse events. Sponsors should include information on these types of discontinuations in addition to information on discontinuations due to adverse events.

rules.rule_text: For drugs with very long half-lives, organ deposition, or potential for irreversible effects, all subjects must be followed to the end of study or after the formal end, and long-term follow-up must include subjects who drop out or finish early.
rules.source_section: Section VI.G: Long-term Follow-up
rules.verbatim_quote: In some cases, it is recommended that all subjects be followed to the end of the study or even after the formal end of the study (e.g., where the drug has a very long half-life, is deposited in an organ such as bone or brain, or has the potential for causing irreversible effects, such as cancer). ... FDA recommends the follow-up for late safety events, even for subjects off therapy, include those subjects who drop out of the trial or who finish the study early due to meeting a primary outcome of interest.
rules.exceptions: Duration of follow-up depends on the circumstances of the product development and should be discussed with the appropriate review division.

rules.rule_text: For a drug that is a new member of an established class of drugs, the adverse events that are important for the class must be fully characterized in the NDA's integrated summary of safety, including analysis of incidence and associated laboratory, vital sign, or ECG data.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H: Important Aspects of Data Presentation
rules.verbatim_quote: For a drug that is a new member of an established class of drugs, the adverse events that are important for the class of drug should be fully characterized in the NDA's integrated summary of safety. That characterization should include an analysis of the incidence of the pertinent adverse events, as well as any associated laboratory, vital sign, or ECG data.

rules.rule_text: The integrated summary of safety must include the distribution of important variables across the pooled data, such as sex, age, extent of exposure, concomitant medical conditions, and concomitant medications.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H: Important Aspects of Data Presentation
rules.verbatim_quote: The distribution of important variables across the pooled data, such as sex, age, extent of exposure, concomitant medical conditions, and concomitant medications (especially those that are used commonly to treat the indication being studied), should be included in the integrated summary of safety.

rules.rule_text: Case report forms submitted for patients who died or discontinued prematurely due to an adverse event must include copies of relevant hospital records, autopsy reports, biopsy reports, and radiological reports where feasible.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H: Important Aspects of Data Presentation
rules.verbatim_quote: Case report forms (CRFs) submitted for patients who died or discontinued a study prematurely due to an adverse event should include copies of relevant hospital records, autopsy reports, biopsy reports, and radiological reports, when feasible.

rules.rule_text: Narrative summaries of important adverse events must provide sufficient detail to permit adequate understanding of the event, including patient age/sex, signs/symptoms, exposure duration, medical history, concomitant medications, test results, differential diagnoses, treatment, re-challenge results, and outcomes.
rules.source_section: Section VI.H: Important Aspects of Data Presentation
rules.verbatim_quote: Narrative summaries (as previously described in guidance) of important adverse events (e.g., deaths, events leading to discontinuation, other serious adverse events) should provide the detail necessary to permit an adequate understanding of the nature of the adverse event experienced by the study subject. ... The following is a list of components that would be found in a useful narrative summary: – Patient age and sex; – Signs and symptoms related to the adverse event being discussed; – An assessment of the relationship of exposure duration to the development of the adverse event; – Pertinent medical history; – Concomitant medications with start dates relative to the adverse event; – Pertinent physical exam findings; – Pertinent test results (e.g., lab data, ECG data, biopsy data); – Discussion of the diagnosis as supported by available clinical data; – For events without a definitive diagnosis, a list of the differential diagnoses; – Treatment provided; – Re-challenge results (if performed); – Outcomes and follow-up information

rules.rule_text: During all risk assessment and risk minimization activities, sponsors must comply with applicable regulatory requirements involving human subjects research and patient privacy, including 45 CFR part 46, 21 CFR parts 50 and 56, and HIPAA.
rules.source_section: Section II.B: Overview of the Risk Management Guidances
rules.verbatim_quote: During all risk assessment and risk minimization activities, sponsors must comply with applicable regulatory requirements involving human subjects research and patient privacy.
